title: Help for Dogs Having Difficulty Rising from a Lying Position
meta_desc: Is your dog struggling to stand? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet diagnose mobility issues.
content_html:
TL;DR: Difficulty rising is typically a sign of chronic pain or mobility issues like arthritis and usually requires a scheduled vet visit rather than emergency care. Recording a video of your dog's movement at home helps your vet see symptoms that may be hidden by adrenaline during an exam.

What does it mean if my dog has difficulty rising?

When a dog has trouble getting up from a resting position, it is often a sign of physical discomfort, joint stiffness, or neurological weakness. This behavior typically manifests as your dog needing several attempts to stand, using their front legs to 'drag' their hindquarters up, or appearing very stiff during their first few steps. While it is common in senior pets, it should always be evaluated to improve their quality of life.

Is my dog's difficulty getting up considered an emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is generally Low, as it is often a chronic, slow-developing issue related to conditions like osteoarthritis or age-related muscle loss.
  • While it requires a veterinary appointment to manage pain and mobility, it is not typically a life-threatening emergency.
  • Immediate veterinary attention is required only if your dog suddenly loses the use of their legs entirely or is in visible respiratory distress.

How can a photo or video help the veterinarian triage my dog?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a video of your dog attempting to rise from a lying position is one of the most helpful tools you can offer.
  • Pets often experience a surge of adrenaline at the clinic which can temporarily mask their stiffness or pain, making them appear more mobile than they are at home.
  • A video allows the vet to observe the natural movement in a stress-free environment, helping them pinpoint exactly which joints or muscle groups are affected.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a dog has difficulty rising, several techniques can be attempted to assist. Clinicians can try manipulating the limbs to encourage weight bearing. In some cases, lifting devices may be used to help the animal stand, ensuring the device is properly padded to prevent injury and is not applied too tightly, allowing circulation to be reestablished. The animal should be lifted slowly until the hindfeet just touch the ground, and assistants can help force each hindlimb into a weight-bearing position by manipulating the stifle and hock.

Chapter: General Principles, Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1191)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of this behavior?
faqs.answer: Common causes include osteoarthritis, hip dysplasia, intervertebral disc disease (IVDD), or general muscle atrophy in senior dogs.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain relief like Ibuprofen?
faqs.answer: No. Human non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are toxic to dogs and can cause life-threatening kidney failure or stomach ulcers. Only use medications prescribed by your vet.

faqs.question: What can I do at home to help my dog stand up?
faqs.answer: Placing non-slip yoga mats or rugs over hardwood floors can provide better traction. You can also use a supportive harness or a towel looped under their belly to gently assist them in standing.
